summaryText: This page has 4 critical issues that will block some users entirely. Fix these first before moving on to the other 15 issues.
keySummary: This page needs major fixes due to aria attribute is not allowed for this role and aria role is missing required child elements.

startHere.title: ARIA attribute is not allowed for this role
startHere.whyItMatters: Each ARIA role only supports certain attributes. Using disallowed ones means the attribute is ignored — and the developer's intent is not communicated.
startHere.whatYouCanDo: Look up the role in the ARIA specification and remove any attributes not listed as supported.

startHere.title: ARIA role is missing required child elements
startHere.whyItMatters: Some roles (like role="list", "tablist", "tree") expect specific child roles to work. Missing them means screen readers cannot announce the structure.
startHere.whatYouCanDo: Check the ARIA specification for the role and ensure the required child roles are present.

startHere.title: Buttons have no readable name
startHere.whyItMatters: If a button contains only an icon or is empty, screen reader users hear "button" with no context about what it does.
startHere.whatYouCanDo: Add visible text inside the button, or use aria-label to provide a description.

gigw: 5.2 – Provide accessible names for all interactive elements

issues.title: ARIA attribute is not allowed for this role
issues.whyItMatters: Each ARIA role only supports certain attributes. Using disallowed ones means the attribute is ignored — and the developer's intent is not communicated.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Look up the role in the ARIA specification and remove any attributes not listed as supported.

issues.title: ARIA role is missing required child elements
issues.whyItMatters: Some roles (like role="list", "tablist", "tree") expect specific child roles to work. Missing them means screen readers cannot announce the structure.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Check the ARIA specification for the role and ensure the required child roles are present.

issues.title: Buttons have no readable name
issues.whyItMatters: If a button contains only an icon or is empty, screen reader users hear "button" with no context about what it does.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add visible text inside the button, or use aria-label to provide a description.

gigw: 5.2 – Provide accessible names for all interactive elements

issues.title: Form fields are missing labels
issues.whyItMatters: Screen readers read out the label when a user focuses on a form field. Without a label, users do not know what to type.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add a <label> element linked to each input using the for attribute, or use aria-label directly on the input.

gigw: 5.1 – Label all form controls explicitly

issues.example:
Before: <input type="text" placeholder="Email" />
After:  <label for="email">Email address</label>
        <input id="email" type="text" />

issues.title: Element uses an ARIA attribute it should not have
issues.whyItMatters: Some elements explicitly forbid certain ARIA attributes — for example, generic elements (role="generic", <span>) should not carry aria-label. Using them silently fails or produces inconsistent screen reader output.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Either change the element's role to one that supports the attribute, or remove the attribute and use a child with text instead.

issues.title: Text is difficult to read (low contrast)
issues.whyItMatters: When text and background colours are too similar, people with low vision or colour blindness struggle to read the content.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Increase the contrast between text and background. Normal text needs a ratio of at least 4.5:1. Use a contrast checker tool to verify.

gigw: 3.3 – Ensure sufficient colour contrast for all text

issues.example:
Before: grey text (#aaa) on white — ratio 2.3:1
After:  dark grey text (#595959) on white — ratio 7:1

issues.title: ARIA input field has no readable name
issues.whyItMatters: Custom inputs built with role="textbox|combobox|searchbox|spinbutton" need an accessible name, otherwise screen readers cannot announce what the field is for.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add aria-label or aria-labelledby pointing to a visible label.

gigw: 5.1 – Label all form controls explicitly

issues.title: SVG graphics are missing descriptions
issues.whyItMatters: SVG icons and graphics that convey meaning must have a text alternative. Otherwise, screen readers either skip them or read raw shape data.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add a <title> child element inside the SVG, or use aria-label on the SVG itself.

gigw: 3.1 – Provide text alternatives for all non-text content

issues.title: Scrollable area cannot be reached by keyboard
issues.whyItMatters: When a region scrolls but has no focusable content and no tabindex, keyboard users cannot scroll it at all — they are stuck.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add tabindex="0" to the scrollable container, or include focusable elements inside it.

issues.title: Interactive elements are nested inside each other
issues.whyItMatters: Nesting buttons inside links (or inputs inside buttons) confuses both screen readers and keyboard users — clicking either parent or child triggers different things, and screen readers may announce only one.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Place interactive elements side by side instead of nested. Restructure the markup so each control is independent.

issues.title: Page has no main content area marked
issues.whyItMatters: Screen reader users can jump straight to the main content by pressing a shortcut key. Without a <main> landmark, they have to listen through the entire navigation first.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Wrap your page's primary content in a <main> element.

gigw: 4.1 – Provide a skip-to-main-content mechanism

issues.title: Page sections are not labelled for navigation
issues.whyItMatters: Landmarks like <header>, <nav>, <main>, and <footer> let keyboard and screen reader users jump between sections quickly.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Wrap major sections in appropriate semantic HTML elements or add role attributes.

issues.title: Headings are not in the right order
issues.whyItMatters: Screen reader users navigate by heading level. Skipping levels (e.g. H1 → H3) breaks the document outline and makes the structure hard to follow.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Use heading levels in sequence: H1 once, then H2 for sections, H3 for subsections, and so on. Do not skip levels.

gigw: 4.4 – Use a logical, hierarchical heading structure

issues.title: Page is missing a main heading
issues.whyItMatters: The main heading (H1) tells users what the page is about. Screen reader users often skim headings to navigate — a missing H1 is disorienting.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add one <h1> element that clearly describes the purpose of the page.

gigw: 4.4 – Use a logical, hierarchical heading structure

issues.example:
Before:

Welcome to our portal

After:

Welcome to the Government Services Portal

issues.title: Page has more than one banner landmark
issues.whyItMatters: A page should have only one banner. Multiple banners confuse screen reader users navigating the landmark list.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Keep one <header> at the page level and remove role="banner" from other elements.

issues.title: Two landmarks have the same role and label
issues.whyItMatters: When two landmarks of the same kind have identical (or no) labels, screen reader users cannot tell them apart in the landmark list.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add a unique aria-label or aria-labelledby to each landmark — e.g. "Main navigation" vs "Footer navigation".

issues.title: Main landmark is nested inside another landmark
issues.whyItMatters: <main> should always sit at the top level of the page. Putting it inside another landmark breaks the assumption that a page has exactly one main content area.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Move the <main> element out of any wrapper landmark and place it at the page's top level.

issues.title: A heading has no text
issues.whyItMatters: An empty heading appears in the screen reader's heading list as a blank entry, breaking page navigation.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Either fill the heading with descriptive text, or remove it entirely.

gigw: 4.4 – Use a logical, hierarchical heading structure

issues.title: Element has a role that is not allowed for that tag
issues.whyItMatters: Some HTML elements should not have certain ARIA roles applied — e.g. <a href> with role="heading" makes it stop working as a link in some screen readers.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Either change the ARIA role to one allowed for this element, or use a different element.

startHere.whatYouCanDo: Look up the role in the ARIA specification and remove any attributes not listed as supported.

startHere.whyItMatters: Some roles (like role="list", "tablist", "tree") expect specific child roles to work. Missing them means screen readers cannot announce the structure.
startHere.whatYouCanDo: Check the ARIA specification for the role and ensure the required child roles are present.

issues.whyItMatters: Custom inputs built with role="textbox|combobox|searchbox|spinbutton" need an accessible name, otherwise screen readers cannot announce what the field is for.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add aria-label or aria-labelledby pointing to a visible label.

gigw: 5.1 – Label all form controls explicitly

issues.title: Page has no main content area marked
issues.whyItMatters: Screen reader users can jump straight to the main content by pressing a shortcut key. Without a <main> landmark, they have to listen through the entire navigation first.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Wrap your page's primary content in a <main> element.

gigw: 4.1 – Provide a skip-to-main-content mechanism

issues.title: Page is missing a main heading
issues.whyItMatters: The main heading (H1) tells users what the page is about. Screen reader users often skim headings to navigate — a missing H1 is disorienting.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add one <h1> element that clearly describes the purpose of the page.

gigw: 4.4 – Use a logical, hierarchical heading structure

issues.example:
Before:

Welcome to our portal

After:

Welcome to the Government Services Portal

issues.title: Page sections are not labelled for navigation
issues.whyItMatters: Landmarks like <header>, <nav>, <main>, and <footer> let keyboard and screen reader users jump between sections quickly.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Wrap major sections in appropriate semantic HTML elements or add role attributes.

startHere.title: ARIA attribute is not allowed for this role
startHere.whyItMatters: Each ARIA role only supports certain attributes. Using disallowed ones means the attribute is ignored — and the developer's intent is not communicated.
startHere.whatYouCanDo: Look up the role in the ARIA specification and remove any attributes not listed as supported.

startHere.title: ARIA role is missing required child elements
startHere.whyItMatters: Some roles (like role="list", "tablist", "tree") expect specific child roles to work. Missing them means screen readers cannot announce the structure.
startHere.whatYouCanDo: Check the ARIA specification for the role and ensure the required child roles are present.

startHere.title: Form fields are missing labels
startHere.whyItMatters: Screen readers read out the label when a user focuses on a form field. Without a label, users do not know what to type.
startHere.whatYouCanDo: Add a <label> element linked to each input using the for attribute, or use aria-label directly on the input.

gigw: 5.1 – Label all form controls explicitly

startHere.example:
Before: <input type="text" placeholder="Email" />
After:  <label for="email">Email address</label>
        <input id="email" type="text" />

issues.title: ARIA attribute is not allowed for this role
issues.whyItMatters: Each ARIA role only supports certain attributes. Using disallowed ones means the attribute is ignored — and the developer's intent is not communicated.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Look up the role in the ARIA specification and remove any attributes not listed as supported.

issues.title: ARIA role is missing required child elements
issues.whyItMatters: Some roles (like role="list", "tablist", "tree") expect specific child roles to work. Missing them means screen readers cannot announce the structure.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Check the ARIA specification for the role and ensure the required child roles are present.

issues.title: Form fields are missing labels
issues.whyItMatters: Screen readers read out the label when a user focuses on a form field. Without a label, users do not know what to type.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add a <label> element linked to each input using the for attribute, or use aria-label directly on the input.

gigw: 5.1 – Label all form controls explicitly

issues.example:
Before: <input type="text" placeholder="Email" />
After:  <label for="email">Email address</label>
        <input id="email" type="text" />

issues.title: Buttons have no readable name
issues.whyItMatters: If a button contains only an icon or is empty, screen reader users hear "button" with no context about what it does.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add visible text inside the button, or use aria-label to provide a description.

gigw: 5.2 – Provide accessible names for all interactive elements

issues.title: Element uses an ARIA attribute it should not have
issues.whyItMatters: Some elements explicitly forbid certain ARIA attributes — for example, generic elements (role="generic", <span>) should not carry aria-label. Using them silently fails or produces inconsistent screen reader output.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Either change the element's role to one that supports the attribute, or remove the attribute and use a child with text instead.

issues.title: Text is difficult to read (low contrast)
issues.whyItMatters: When text and background colours are too similar, people with low vision or colour blindness struggle to read the content.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Increase the contrast between text and background. Normal text needs a ratio of at least 4.5:1. Use a contrast checker tool to verify.

gigw: 3.3 – Ensure sufficient colour contrast for all text

issues.example:
Before: grey text (#aaa) on white — ratio 2.3:1
After:  dark grey text (#595959) on white — ratio 7:1

issues.title: Interactive elements are nested inside each other
issues.whyItMatters: Nesting buttons inside links (or inputs inside buttons) confuses both screen readers and keyboard users — clicking either parent or child triggers different things, and screen readers may announce only one.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Place interactive elements side by side instead of nested. Restructure the markup so each control is independent.

issues.title: ARIA input field has no readable name
issues.whyItMatters: Custom inputs built with role="textbox|combobox|searchbox|spinbutton" need an accessible name, otherwise screen readers cannot announce what the field is for.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add aria-label or aria-labelledby pointing to a visible label.

gigw: 5.1 – Label all form controls explicitly

issues.title: Page has no main content area marked
issues.whyItMatters: Screen reader users can jump straight to the main content by pressing a shortcut key. Without a <main> landmark, they have to listen through the entire navigation first.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Wrap your page's primary content in a <main> element.

gigw: 4.1 – Provide a skip-to-main-content mechanism

issues.title: Page is missing a main heading
issues.whyItMatters: The main heading (H1) tells users what the page is about. Screen reader users often skim headings to navigate — a missing H1 is disorienting.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add one <h1> element that clearly describes the purpose of the page.

gigw: 4.4 – Use a logical, hierarchical heading structure

issues.whatYouCanDo: Add aria-label or aria-labelledby pointing to a visible label.

issues.title: Main landmark is nested inside another landmark
issues.whyItMatters: <main> should always sit at the top level of the page. Putting it inside another landmark breaks the assumption that a page has exactly one main content area.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Move the <main> element out of any wrapper landmark and place it at the page's top level.

issues.title: Page is missing a main heading
issues.whyItMatters: The main heading (H1) tells users what the page is about. Screen reader users often skim headings to navigate — a missing H1 is disorienting.
issues.whatYouCanDo: Add one <h1> element that clearly describes the purpose of the page.

gigw: 4.4 – Use a logical, hierarchical heading structure

Before:

Welcome to our portal

After:
